= CyborgDesigner =

== Summary Of Features ==

Overview

'''CyborgDesigner''' is a visual layout editor that simplifies the process of building complex user interfaces and other layouts. 

It uses a metadata-driven approach, where the user's actions are translated into changes in a hierarchical data model. The application features a draggable component palette and a dynamic canvas that updates in real-time to reflect the underlying data structure. This allows users to intuitively design forms, dashboards, and other layouts without writing a single line of code.

External Link: [https://cyborgunicorn.com.au/|Cyborg Unicorn Website]

[[Image:cyborgdesigner.jpg|Layout Editor]]


- - - -

== Core Concepts ==

CyborgDesigner is based on two core concepts: '''Datatypes''' and '''Layout Designers'''. These are defined in the initial configuration and drive the entire application.

'''Datatypes''': 

These are the fundamental components of a layout. They represent individual form fields like text boxes, checkboxes, or rich text editors. These components are available in the sidebar palette and can be dragged onto the canvas.

'''Layout Designers''': 

These are the tools used to organize and structure datatypes. They act as containers and include layoutsections, horizontalcontainers, and verticalcontainers.


- - - -

== Metadata Structure ==

The entire layout is stored as a JavaScript object in a hierarchical format. This object is the single source of truth for the application.

'''Layout Sections''': 

The top-level element is a layout section (layoutsection), which organizes the layout into distinct areas. You can consider a layout section in the context of a form as being part of a form, it could be an optional part or just a part that you may want to group fields together within.

'''Containers''': 

A section contains one or more containers (horizontalcontainer or verticalcontainer). A horizontalcontainer can only contain other verticalcontainers, allowing for a multi-column layout.

'''Fields''': 

A verticalcontainer contains the individual field objects, which correspond to the datatypes.

The application's rendering engine constantly converts this metadata into HTML, ensuring the UI is always synchronized with the data.

<code>
// Example of a form layout
[
	{ 
		id: section-1, 
		caption: 'Form Header',
		containers: [
			{
				id: container-1,
				type: 'verticalcontainer',
				caption: 'Form Header Container',
				name: 'FORMHEADER',
				label: 'Form Header',
				children: [
					{ id: field-1, type: 'textbox', caption: 'Entity Name', label: 'Entity Name', name: 'ENTITYNAME', container: false },
					{ id: field-2, type: 'number', caption: 'Form Version', label: 'Form Version', name: 'FORMVERSION', container: false },
					{ id: field-3, type: 'textbox', caption: 'Code', label: 'Code', name: 'CODE', container: false },
					{ id: field-4, type: 'textbox', caption: 'Description', label: 'Description', name: 'DESCRIPTION', container: false },
					{ id: field-5, type: 'checkbox', caption: 'Is Enabled?', label: 'Is Enabled?', name: 'ISENABLED', container: false }
				]
			}
		]
	},
	{ 
		id: section-2, 
		caption: 'Data Header',
		containers: [
			{
				id: container-2,
				type: 'verticalcontainer',
				caption: 'Vertical Container',
				name: 'DATAHEADER',
				label: 'Data Header',
				children: [
					{ id: field-6, type: 'number', caption: 'Data Version', label: 'Data Version', name: 'DATAVERSION', container: false }
				]
			}
		]
	},
	{ 
		id: section-3, 
		caption: 'Form Data',
		containers: [
			{
				id: container-3,
				type: 'verticalcontainer',
				caption: 'Vertical Container',
				name: 'FORMDATA',
				label: 'Form Data',
				children: [
					{ id: field-7, type: 'textbox', caption: 'Code', label: 'Code', name: 'CODE', container: false },
					{ id: field-8, type: 'textbox', caption: 'Description', label: 'Description', name: 'DESCRIPTION', container: false },
					{ id: field-9, type: 'checkbox', caption: 'Is Enabled?', label: 'Is Enabled?', name: 'ISENABLED', container: false }
				]
			}
		]
	}
]
</code>

- - - -

== Event Handling ==

The user interface is powered by a robust event handling system that translates user actions into changes in the layout metadata. The core of this system is the drag-and-drop functionality.

== Drag & Drop ==

When a user drags a component from the sidebar or an existing component on the canvas, the event system sets a data payload in the format of type:id (e.g., datatype:textbox or existingcontainer:container-1). When the component is dropped, a specialized handler function processes the data and performs the following actions:

'''Reordering''': 

Moves an existing component to a new position.

'''Nesting''': 

Adds a component as a child of a valid parent (e.g., a field in a vertical container).

'''Creation''': 

Creates a new component object with a unique ID and adds it to the metadata.

The system intelligently handles complex scenarios, such as reordering a container by dropping it on a field, or adding a new section by dropping a component in a blank area.

== Other Events ==

'''Click''': 

Selects a component and populates the properties panel with its editable attributes, allowing the user to customize captions, IDs, and other settings.

'''Double-Click''': 

A shortcut that selects a component and immediately navigates to the properties panel.

'''Remove''': 

Deletes a component from the metadata and the UI.

Any data types and their properties can be configured, which means the application is not hard-coded to a specific set of fields. This is achieved by defining the components and their attributes in a simple, customizable data structure, usually a JavaScript object or JSON file.


- - - -

== Configuration Data Structure ==

The system uses a configurable data structure to define the available components. The m_objOptions object holds the core configuration, including datatypes, nativeDatatypes, and containers.

'''datatypes''': 

An array of custom components you define. Each object in this array represents a distinct field type (e.g., a "Product Picker" or "Custom Chart").

'''nativeDatatypes''': 

An array for standard HTML input types, such as "Text Box" or "Checkbox". This allows the application to handle both custom and standard fields with a unified approach.

'''containers''': 

An array that defines the structural components used for layout (e.g., "Layout Section" and "Horizontal Container").

Each object in these arrays is a schema that dictates how the component behaves and what properties it exposes.

<code>
// Example of a custom datatype configuration
{ 
	type: 'sequence', 
	caption: 'Sequence', 
	container: false,
	properties: [
		{ name: 'name', label: 'Name', datatype: 'nativetextbox' },
		{ name: 'label', label: 'Label', datatype: 'nativetextbox' },
		{ name: 'classes', label: 'Classes', datatype: 'nativetextbox' },
		{ name: 'value', label: 'Value', datatype: 'nativetextbox' },
		{ name: 'length', label: 'Length', datatype: 'nativetextbox' },
		{ name: 'required', label: 'Required', datatype: 'nativeyesno' },
		{ name: 'readonly', label: 'Read Only', datatype: 'nativeyesno' },
		{ name: 'searchable', label: 'Searchable', datatype: 'nativeyesno' },
		{ name: 'tooltip', label: 'Tool Tip', datatype: 'nativetextbox' },
		{ name: 'metadata', label: 'Meta Data', datatype: 'nativemultilinetextbox' }
	]
}
</code>

- - - -

== Dynamic Properties Panel ==

The most significant benefit of this configurable approach is the dynamic properties panel. When a user selects a component on the canvas, the application looks up its type in the configured datatypes or nativeDatatypes arrays. It then reads the properties array from that component's schema and dynamically generates the input fields in the properties panel.

This means you can:

Add new properties to existing components by simply adding a new object to their properties array.

Create entirely new components by adding a new object to the datatypes array, complete with its own set of unique properties.

Control input types for each property, using datatypes like nativetextbox or nativeyesno to ensure the user provides valid data.

This design makes the system highly extensible. A developer can add new custom components with unique configurations and behaviors without having to modify the core logic of the designer itself.

- - - -
Powered by %APPNAME%
